meta.title: 1.3 The Prototype-to-Production Gap
meta.description: Learn what actually breaks when a user tries to ship a Make app — authentication, database migrations, error handling, secrets management, and scale — and why Make's design system context gives it a shorter prototype-to-production gap than competitors starting from scratch.

blocks.prompt:
> A user builds a working app in Make in 30 minutes. They show it to their boss, who says "ship it." What breaks?

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module enumerates the concrete systems that fail between a working demo and a production app — authentication, database migrations, error handling, secrets management, and scale. By the end, you'll understand why this gap can't be closed by better prompting, why auth integration is safer than auth generation for non-technical users, and how Make's design system context gives it a shorter prototype-to-production gap than competitors starting from scratch.

The prototype-to-production gap is not a model quality problem — it is a systems problem comprising authentication, error handling, scale, and trust that requires the platform to either solve programmatically (through integrations and guardrails) or expose explicitly (through developer-grade code visibility and review). Make's demo magic is real — the speed, the quality, the "I can't believe I built that" moment. But the distance between that demo and a production application is not a gap you can bridge with a better prompt. It is a structural problem with how AI-generated code is produced and how production applications must behave.

**The Demo Cliff**

The demo cliff is the specific set of failures that occur when a sandbox application hits the real world. It is not a metaphor — it is a list of concrete systems that Make's generated code does not address by default.

**Authentication**

Most demos use hardcoded data or mock users. A production app requires real authentication: user registration, login, password reset, session management, and authorization (not just who you are, but what you're allowed to do). Authentication is not a component — it is a system that touches every route, every database query, and every API call.

AI-generated authentication code is particularly dangerous. Subtle mistakes in session management (not rotating tokens, not setting secure/httpOnly flags on cookies — browser flags that prevent JavaScript from reading cookies, protecting against cross-site scripting attacks, not validating JWT claims — JWT stands for JSON Web Token, a signed credential that proves a user's identity) create security vulnerabilities that are undetectable without security expertise. Make's platform must decide: does it generate auth code, or does it integrate with auth providers (Auth.js, Clerk, Supabase Auth) that are maintained by security experts?

**Database and Migrations**

Demo apps often use in-memory arrays or localStorage. A production app requires a real database with a schema, indexes, constraints, and a migration system. Database migrations (scripts that modify an existing database schema — adding columns, renaming tables, changing types — without losing existing data) are one of the highest-risk operations in software — a bad migration can corrupt production data irreversibly.

Generated SQL migrations need to be reviewed by a human who understands the data model. Make cannot generate a migration and run it against a production database without human review. This is a hard limit on the agent's autonomy.

**Error Handling and Empty States**

Demos use the happy path. Production apps need to handle:
- Network failures and timeouts
- Invalid user input
- Empty states (no data yet, no results found)
- Unexpected server errors
- Rate limiting
- Concurrent access conflicts

Generated code often has optimistic error handling — it assumes the happy path. In production, the unhappy path is a feature.

blocks.question: The module says Make must decide whether to generate auth code or integrate with auth providers like Clerk or Supabase Auth. What are the tradeoffs of each approach, and which is the right default for Make's non-technical user segment?
blocks.answer: Generating auth code gives the user full control and no external dependencies, but AI-generated authentication has a high error rate for subtle security issues (improper token validation, missing cookie flags, no rate limiting on login attempts). The generated code looks correct but may have invisible security gaps. Integrating with proven auth providers (Clerk, Supabase Auth, Auth.js) means the platform doesn't generate auth logic — it generates the integration code to call a trusted service. For non-technical users, this is clearly the right default: they cannot audit security code and will deploy whatever the AI generates. The integration approach shifts security responsibility to a specialized provider with a track record. For developers who want custom auth, an escape hatch to generate code directly is still valuable — but it should not be the default for a non-technical builder persona.

blocks.content:
**Environment Variables and Secrets**

Demo code often has API keys hardcoded in the source. Production apps must externalize secrets to environment variables (runtime-injected values that configure an app without being embedded in the code — the standard way to handle API keys, database URLs, and other secrets) and never commit them to source control. Make's generated code must be audited for hardcoded credentials before deployment. Better: the platform should prevent generated code from including secrets at all.

**Scale**

A demo that works for one user may fail for 1,000. Database queries without indexes are fast on 100 rows; they timeout on 10 million. API calls made in a loop (N+1 query problem — a performance antipattern where loading N items triggers N additional queries instead of one batched query) work in a demo; they bring down a server in production. Generated code does not automatically include pagination, caching, or query optimization.

**The Trust Problem**

Would you deploy AI-generated code to production without reading it? Experienced developers say no — they read every line, understand what it does, and take responsibility for it. Non-technical users say yes — that's why they're using an AI tool.

This creates a trust gradient. The platform must accommodate both:
- For developers: expose the code, make it reviewable, make it editable, make it auditable
- For non-technical users: abstract the code, make the output verifiable through behavior (not reading), provide guardrails that catch common production failures

## How to Think About This

The demo cliff is not a list of missing features — it is a list of categories of risk that AI models are not trained to prevent. Auth, error handling, secrets management, and scale each fail silently: the app looks and feels fine in a demo, but is broken in ways that only manifest under real conditions (multiple users, bad input, unexpected network failures). The platform's job is to either prevent these failures programmatically (integrate with auth providers, detect hardcoded secrets, add error boundary templates) or surface them explicitly before deployment, so users can make an informed choice rather than an unwitting one.

**The Core Tension of the Role**

Make's product promise is democratizing software creation. Its production aspiration requires the rigor of professional software engineering. These are in tension. Every feature decision is a tradeoff between:
- Simplicity (hide complexity, lower the floor) and power (expose tools, raise the ceiling)
- Speed (generate and ship) and safety (review and verify)
- Automation (agent decides) and control (human decides)

The Platform PM's job is not to resolve this tension — it is to manage where on each dimension Make sits for each user segment, and to ensure the infrastructure supports the full range without the floor collapsing.

**The Make-Specific Opportunity**

Most competitors face the prototype-to-production gap without design system context. Make has a structural advantage: it knows the user's Figma component library. This means generated code can use the real design system — production-ready components, not one-off approximations. A design system component is already tested, accessible, and responsive. AI-generated code using the design system starts closer to production than code generated from scratch.

This is the argument for Make's platform investment: not just faster generation, but higher-quality generation because of design context — which means a shorter prototype-to-production gap than competitors can achieve.

---

blocks.question: A PM on the Make team proposes: "We should add a 'Production Readiness Score' that rates generated apps 1–100 before deploy." What would go into that score, and what's the risk of adding it to the product?
blocks.answer:
Good inputs for a readiness score: presence of error handling, auth implementation, no hardcoded credentials, database migration files present, input validation, environment variable usage, responsive layout, loading/empty states. The risk: (1) false confidence — a score of 85 doesn't mean the app is ready for production, it means it scores well on a rubric; (2) score-chasing — the AI or the user optimizes for the score rather than for actual production readiness; (3) liability — if Figma publishes a score and the app fails in production, the trust damage is worse than if no score existed. A better PM framing: a checklist with human confirmation, not an opaque score.

---

blocks.title:
Take a Lovable or Bolt-generated app and do a production readiness audit. Checklist: (1) Are there hardcoded credentials? (2) Is there auth? (3) Does the app handle network failure gracefully? (4) Are there empty states? (5) Does it work on mobile? (6) What happens if you submit the form with invalid data? Count how many items pass. This is the prototype-to-production gap made concrete.

---
blocks.solution: Run a simple app through all six checklist items. In most AI-generated apps, you'll find: (1) Hardcoded credentials — common in generated apps that call external APIs; look for strings matching patterns like `sk-`, `Bearer `, or obvious key names in the source code; (2) Auth — almost always absent in demos unless you explicitly asked for it; the app likely has no login screen, no session management, and no route protection; (3) Network failure handling — typically missing; disconnect your network and reload the page — most generated apps show a white screen or JavaScript error rather than a graceful offline message; (4) Empty states — hit or miss; try deleting all data from the app and see if it shows a helpful 'No items yet' message or a blank component; (5) Mobile — resize the browser to 375px wide; Tailwind apps often pass this check if the AI used responsive classes, but many don't; (6) Invalid form input — submit with empty required fields, enter a non-email in an email field, enter a negative number in a positive-only field; most generated apps either accept invalid input silently or crash. A typical audit result: 1–2 items pass out of 6. This is not a failure of the AI — it is an accurate representation of the prototype-to-production gap. The gap isn't about code quality; it's about which concerns the AI was asked to address.

blocks.prompt:
Explain to a Figma design leader why "works in preview" and "production-ready" are not the same thing — using one concrete failure mode that would affect a designer's own users if they shipped a Make app.

---

questions: Think of a product you shipped where the demo worked flawlessly but something broke in production that wasn't caught in testing. What category of failure was it — auth, scale, error handling, secrets — and what did it reveal about your team's pre-launch checklist?

When you've worked on products or platforms that serve users who can't debug errors themselves — customers, non-technical stakeholders, end consumers — how did your team decide what guardrails to build versus what to leave to the user's judgment? What carried over from that experience to how you'd think about Make's safety responsibility?

---

blocks.content:
The prototype-to-production gap is not a model quality problem — it is a systems problem comprising authentication, error handling, scale, and trust that requires the platform to either solve programmatically (through integrations and guardrails) or expose explicitly (through developer-grade code visibility and review).

---

blocks.prompt:
> The gap is structural. But it affects different users differently — developers can bridge it themselves, non-technical builders cannot, and AI agents close it in ways that may introduce new risks. The user segment analysis is next.

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: Why does the module recommend that Make integrate with established auth providers (Clerk, Supabase Auth) rather than generating authentication code from scratch?
options: Generating auth code is technically impossible for current AI models because authentication requires real user accounts

AI-generated auth code can look correct but contain invisible security vulnerabilities — improper token rotation, missing httpOnly cookie flags, unvalidated JWT claims — that non-technical users cannot detect before deploying
Auth providers are faster to integrate than generated code because they provide pre-built UI components
reviewQuestions.explanation: The danger with AI-generated authentication is that the failure mode is invisible. Code that runs without errors can still be insecure if session tokens aren't rotated after privilege escalation, if cookies lack the httpOnly flag, or if JWT claims aren't properly validated. These issues don't cause runtime errors — they cause security breaches. Non-technical users who deploy Make apps cannot audit this code. Integrating with proven providers shifts security responsibility to teams with the expertise to maintain it.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the invisible failure mode is what makes generated auth code dangerous at scale, particularly for users who can't evaluate it.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: AI models can generate auth code — the problem is that it may look correct but be subtly insecure. Missing httpOnly flags on cookies, improper JWT validation, and failure to rotate tokens don't cause visible errors; they create security vulnerabilities. Since non-technical users can't audit the code before deploying, the platform's safer default is integration with providers who specialize in security.

reviewQuestions.question: The N+1 query problem is a performance antipattern relevant to Make's prototype-to-production gap. Which of the following correctly describes it?
options: Loading N items triggers N additional database queries instead of one batched query — a pattern that works in a demo but can bring down a production server at scale
An app that runs correctly on one user's machine fails on N other machines because of environment variable differences
Generating N components in one prompt produces output where the first component is correct and subsequent ones have errors
reviewQuestions.explanation: The N+1 problem occurs when code loads a list of N items and then makes an additional database (or API) call for each item individually, instead of fetching all related data in a single batched query. With 10 items in a demo, this produces 11 queries — imperceptible. With 10,000 items in production, it produces 10,001 queries that can exhaust database connections and cause timeouts. AI-generated code often produces this pattern because demos with small datasets don't surface it.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Good — the N+1 problem is a classic demo-to-production failure mode where scale makes an invisible inefficiency visible and catastrophic.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The N+1 problem is specifically a database/API query pattern: loading N items triggers N separate follow-up queries instead of one batched query. In a small demo it's invisible; at production scale it collapses performance. It's not about multi-machine deployment or prompt generation quality.

reviewQuestions.question: What does the module identify as the 'trust gradient' for deploying AI-generated code, and which user segment sits at each end?
options: The trust gradient runs from free tier users (low trust, no review) to enterprise users (high trust, mandatory review process)

Developers review generated code before deploying and take ownership of it; non-technical users deploy without reading the code — requiring the platform to provide automated safety guardrails for the latter
The trust gradient is about AI model confidence: high-confidence outputs are deployed automatically, low-confidence outputs require human sign-off
reviewQuestions.explanation: The trust gradient is defined by user capability to evaluate code. Experienced developers don't automatically trust AI output — they read every line, verify behavior, and take responsibility. Non-technical users deploy whatever the AI produces because they have no means to evaluate it. These opposite defaults require opposite platform designs: developers need code visibility and escape hatches; non-technical users need automated guardrails that catch production failures before they reach real users.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — the trust gradient is about evaluation capability, and the two ends require opposite platform investments.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The trust gradient isn't about pricing tiers or model confidence scores. It's about who can read and evaluate the generated code before deployment. Developers distrust AI output by default and review it; non-technical users trust it implicitly because they lack the skill to do otherwise. The platform must serve both ends — code visibility for one, automated safety guardrails for the other.
